sentences.text: My name is Henry Dressler.
It's Henry-- H-E-N-R-Y.
Dressler-- D as in David, R-E-S-S-L-E-R.
I'm sorry, my name at--
At first?
At first.
My name was Heinz Dressler.
H-E-I-N-Z.
Dressler.
D-R-E-S-S-L-E-R.
Not that I know of.
I was born in 1919.
Yeah.
October 22, 1919 in Dresden, Germany.
I'm 75 years old now.
My father's name was Joachim.
That's J-0-A-C-H-I-M.
He had a middle name that he rarely used-- Samuel, Dressler.
D-R-E-S-S-L-E-R.
My mother's name was Martha-- M-A-R-T-H-A-- Dressler.
I had one sister.
Her name was Susi, S-U-S-I.
She was born October 1st, 1914.
He had a business manufacturing domestic linen, tablecloth, things like this, mostly for department stores.
I do not know it-- I do not remember-- relatively small-- relatively small business.
Between five and 10, I would say.
She was active-- actively involved in it.
She worked with him.
Oh, Dresden was a very beautiful city.
It had about 500,000 inhabitants, about 5,000 Jews, if I remember right.
It was, in olden ages, was the seat of the Kingdom of Saxony.
It had some beautiful buildings.
It was a city that was well-known for its beauty, and it was a nice city to live in.
Well, I was born in the house in which we-- I-- we lived in-- I lived there.
As long as I lived in Dresden, I lived in this house.
The address was 34 Struvestrasse.
That's S-T-R-U-V-E-S-T-R-A-S-S-E.
Struvestrasse.
It was off one of the main streets of Dresden, which was Prager Strasse.
P, as in Pete, like the city of Prague.
P-A-R-A-G-E-R Strasse.
It was a nice, residential street of mostly four-story apartment houses.
That was more or less a norm for apartment-house buildings, and we lived on the second floor.
We had-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had about four bed-- four-- four bedrooms, and there was a big dining room, a big living room, and a men's room, which we would call the library also.
And we had three bathrooms.
And we had a balcony in the front and in the back.
It-- it was a nice apartment.
We were-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were normal.
Not overly religious, and not-- not underly [LAUGHS] religiously observant.
We went to temple at the holidays.
Sometimes we went to temple on Friday night or Saturday.
We were very conscious Jews.
Our friends were mixed, and a lot of Jews in it.
And I had Hebrew lessons, which was obligatory in-- in school.
Usually one week-- one day a week on-- on Hebrew, and one day a week on Jewish history.
No, it was not.
Well, we-- my father's parents lived in Dresden, and Passover was a big holiday in their house, where all the family, some from Berlin, came over.
It was a nice holiday and there were usually about 25, 30 people.
And the big holidays, that's-- that's where we were.
Well, first, I had a Seder [INAUDIBLE], of course, when I was younger.

And then my grandfather-- who was at that time in-- in his 70s, but to us he was an old man with a little, white goatee, and they were a little hard of hearing-- and he sort of dominated the-- the table.
My father had an-- had a brother.
His name was Ferdinand, and I was sort of close to him.
And he had another brother who died very young.
I didn't really know him.
And he had two sisters.
One of them lived in Dresden.
These three, four, we were-- we saw a lot.

And while-- while my parents-- when I went to high school, my parents were on business-- I usually went from school by train-- by trolley-- to my grandparents' house, and I ate dinner or lunch, whatever you call it, there.
So I spent a lot of time in their house.
When I was six years old, I went into what you would call your grammar school for four years.
From there, I went to-- it was called [GERMAN] Gymnasium, the King George High School, which was a-- we called it in Germany a humanistic high school, dedicated to pre-college education.
And the subjects where mostly more scientific.
Our first language was English.
It was sort of [INAUDIBLE] English.
The second language would have been-- Latin.
And the third language, Greek.
Ask me what were my un-favorite subjects in school.
They are less.
[LAUGHS] I loved-- I loved geography.
I didn't like biology, which was biology-- the-- the-- in German it was the science of plants and trees and things that grow, not necessarily life.
I didn't like zoology.
I didn't like chemistry, but I liked physics.
I liked Math.
I liked arithmetic.
I liked geography very, very much, and-- and-- language.
Well, in grammar school, we had only one teacher for the-- for the whole four years that I went there.
Her name was Mrs.
Richter, [LAUGHS] R-I-C-H-T-E-R.
And-- and in high school, we had different teachers for different subjects.
I don't think-- the system was a little different than it was here.
No, I didn't have any-- any favorite teacher.
Yeah.
Well, this was the time that Hitler came to power in 1933, when I was 13 years old.
So there-- it was open already.
It would have been in the commercial field somewhere, or in the business field.

Well, after three years-- Hitler came to power in 1933-- after three years, I had to leave that high school because they would not have anymore Jews there, and I was admitted to a-- a private high school that was run by the Chamber of Commerce of Dresden.
And that was a very much business-oriented high school.
They started, back again, with English, but as a business language.

And then it was English, French, and Spanish as business correspondence, and commercial arithmetic, rather than the-- the higher-- higher mathematics and-- and subjects-- bookkeeping, typing, short hand, and business law, and subjects that will help you in a-- in a education-- in a-- in a commercial field.
[SIGHS] Well, there were some teachers-- yes.
There was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had one teacher that taught biology, which was not one of my favorite subjects.
And-- where I was always very good in school.
I was always in the top one, two, or three in the class.
And as a Jew, you could not be the first.
They call it primus, the prime student because we sat in that order.
I could not be the-- the-- the speaker for the class, so I was second or third, and I had all good ones or two grades.
But in-- in biology I had a-- a four, the equivalent, whatever it was.
And one day, the-- the biology teacher spoke up in the class and he comments that I got a bad grade because I was Jewish.
He wanted to hear from me if this is true.
His name was professor Bayer, B-A-Y-E-R.
And of course, I had to get up and say, no, I am bad.
But I was not a good student.
But that was really the only thing that they did.
And the other thing was that we had a lot of assemblies, and during Hitler time, of course, a lot of German songs, the German national anthem, and the and the other Hitler songs were being played.
And I didn't like this too much.
And where-- whenever you had to greet, you had to raise your arm.
And so I played the kettle-- kettle drums in the school orchestra, and I had a good excuse not to have to stand up and raise my arm, sing with them.
I just did my kettle-drums.
Yeah.
Yeah.
No, no.
There were very few students.
In high-- during my whole high school times, there was only about maybe two Jewish students in a class.
That was all.
Well, [LAUGHS] I couldn't really answer that question.
We knew what-- we read the papers, of course, and we started hearing about the restrictions, about the things that started going on.
I want to tell you, in the high school, there was a-- we were not allowed, as Jews, to go in the public swimming pool, even though this was built by a Jewish philanthropist in the city.
We were excluded from-- from certain outings.
Every year, they had a-- an annual like a-- like a little Olympic Games between all the-- between all the high schools.
Each schools marched in the stadium, and it was a contest in the different subjects of-- of sports, different types of sports, and they marched around with our school colors.
We were excluded to participate or even to watch.
And as 12, 13 year olds, something, that's something that hurt.
Well, I was a-- I loved to ride the bicycle.
I did a lot of bicycle riding.
I belonged to a Jewish sports club, [NON-ENGLISH] which I was not very active.
I'm not a sports person.
And I didn't really do too much.
I was mostly at home.
We had a very close family life, and that's where we spent out spare time.
It was different than here.
The children were with their parents much more at that time.
Well, in Germany, I could not have dated a non-Jewish girl.
That was out of the question.
I would run the danger of being-- being arrested, or worse.
There were the racial laws where-- where Jews were not allowed to marry-- marry non-Jews, or vice versa, and it was very strict.
And this was just out of the question.
So I didn't have much of a social life like a 15 or 16 year old would have here, no.
Yes.
Yes.
Well, I remember-- after-- of the campaign I really do not remember too much.
After he was elected, there was a big demonstration in Dresden.
What do you call that?
A [GERMAN], a torch light parade through the whole town.
And everybody torch-lighted at night.
And there must have been hundred thousands of people.
And this, I remember.
It was scary.
Well, we were all concerned.
We were all worried because-- but you know, whatever happened later, nobody had any idea that anything like this could happen.
[INAUDIBLE] I had a-- a-- one [INAUDIBLE], my father had a brother in Berlin who was a-- who had just become a doctor, and who was married to a non-Jewish woman.
And he just had graduated.
And he put his shingle out on April 1st, 1933.
That was three months after Hitler came to power, and it was a day in which all Jewish offices and all Jewish businesses and stores were boycotted.
There was one SR man, the brownshirts, stood in front of the door.
Here, this is a Jewish business.
And the day he put out his shingle, his office was boycotted, and he was smart.
He closed up his office the same day and went to Palestine.
And-- but otherwise, you keep on living your life because you hope-- you hope for the best.
You hope you'll keep on living and things will settle down and-- and straighten out.
Well, we did.
I think everybody did.
But in order to do this-- it was not easy.
The-- the-- America had the immigration quotas going by-- by place of birth, and they were-- they were very limited, and, like, the German quota was very tight.
And you had to have somebody in the United States who would sponsor you.
In other countries, some were-- most of them were difficult to get in.
Shanghai was open.
Cuba was open.
I think Argentina was open.
And there's one other thing you must not forget.
If you live in a country-- you live all your life there, and you have your family there, and you have your business there, your friends there.
You have your roots there.

It is not easy to get yourself up and break up everything you have, only in fear of something worse happening, and start completely new in a country where you probably did not speak the language, where you tear apart the family.
This is probably the most difficult thing for people.
Not everybody can do this.
Yes.
One of my father's sisters went to-- to Palestine.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One of my father's brother went to Shanghai.
Let me go up past-- [INAUDIBLE] may go up to '38.
One of my mother's brothers went to Palestine.
One of my mother's sisters-- brothers went to South America.
And these were really the only people-- and that uncle doctor who went to Israel.
These were on the real-- only people from our family who survived, who-- who were able to get out of Germany.
All the others, they were gone.
We have a couple of cousins left there, but from the direct uncles, this is all there was.
We were in business-- he was in business.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a relatively small business, and he had his customers.
And I have to tell you of an incident that happened.
This was very strange.
My father had a customer who was-- he lived out of town, a small town outside of Dresden, and he came in once a week in the morning.
He brought in material, and he wanted some-- 100 bed sheets, or 300 table cloths, or what done, whatever it was.
And he-- and he went back home, and my father and his people worked all day and night.
And at midnight he went to the railroad station, and put the package on the train, and sent it to them.
Well, he came down.
I don't remember.
It was either Erev Yom Kippur or Erev Rosh Hashanah.
He didn't know we were Jewish.
And he was a good customer, and you had to try to keep-- keep your customers.
He brought in an order to make swastikas-- flags.
He brought in a sample swastika and made-- gave him the order.
You buy the material, you design it, you make it, you put it on the train tonight.
And it was quite a difficult decision to do.
First of all, we had never done this.
And my father did it with-- with-- with tears in his eyes, but it was done.
It's an experience we did not--
[INAUDIBLE] ?
What year?
Must have been 1936 or 1937.
No, it was not.
When we were thrown out of Germany, my father's sister, who-- who lived in Cologne, was visiting with us.
And they-- she was staying with us.
When they took us away, they-- they kept her there, and she closed everything up, and that-- that was it.
Well, I was young, and as a young fellow, you-- you don't take it that seriously.
We could not go to the movies.
Came a point where we could go to the movies.
We could not go to a theater.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We could not go swimming.
We could go swim in the river, but not-- not in a-- in a bath, in a public bath.
And I joined a small social club.
It got together once-- once in a-- once a month, just talking about different things.
And all these club meetings had to be reported to the-- to the Gestapo, and for every club meeting, a civilian SS man or [INAUDIBLE] was present there to-- to see what we had to talk about it.
And you were not free.
You just were not free.
There were other things that didn't personally affect me.
There were a lot of arrests, and disappearances, and the first words about concentration camps became known.
And-- but these were, at this point, still once removed.
I-- I do not remember.
I don't.
I probably-- I'm sure we knew some, but I can't remember.
[SIGHS] I don't know.
I am not aware of it.
Well, I went to high school there, and I was-- I loved geometry.
So I loved to-- to-- to design the-- the five circles, and squares, and then the different colors, the-- interlock.
I liked this very much.
Otherwise, I was not-- never very much sports minded, and I-- so I have no special ideas about this.
Well, I left high school in 1936 with the medium graduation level, and I got a job in a bank.
It was a private, Jewish-owned bank in the center of town.
I was an apprentice with a three-year contract from the chamber of commerce.
That's the way they worked it.
And I went through every department of the bank-- three months in this, then three months in this department.
It was-- of course, we had no computers.
We didn't even-- there was one adding machine in the whole bank that was the head bookkeeper.
Even the-- the tellers had no adding machines.
Everything was done by hand, and it's good brain training, by-- by the way.
And I worked there until 1938.
The bank was Aryanized, taken over by Germans, by a German bank.
And I had to leave, like all the other Jewish people.
And I got a job in a depart-- very fine department store in Dresden, mostly exclusively for women and some fine domestics.
It had a fur department.
It was very, very fine.
I would compare it to Saks Fifth Avenue, but on a much smaller basis.
Hirsch, H-I-R-S-C-H.
Hirsch and Company.
And they were in Prager Strasse, P-R-A-G-E-R Strasse, which was the main street that connected the railroad to the-- to the center of town.
September of '38, we were-- I do not exactly-- remember the exact dates.
September, '38 we were-- my father-- my sister was engaged at that point to a fellow from Berlin, and he called her.
There is going to be something.
It's going to happen.
But you-- you don't know what.
My parents were still in business.
I think it was September '29.
I was home alone.
The police knocked-- somebody knocked at the door.
It was the police.
You have to come to the police station.
Well, I said, I have to wait for my parents.
And they sat down with us and waited.
And eating was always very important to me.
When they came, I said, well, I have to eat dinner first.
[LAUGHS] They waited, and then they left my aunt here, but they took us to the police station.
And there were other Jews assembled, and they-- they took us to a large hall, like a ballroom hall, and we had to spend the night there.
Then we were told we were going to be sent out next morning.
And next morning, they put us all on a train.
They took us by-- the police had these big open buses, these troop-- troop carriers, called it.
They drove us through the whole town, so everybody could see us Jews being transported by the police, and they took us to the-- to the railroad station.
And there was a train set aside for us, about 1,500 people, and 1,400 or 1,500 people from Dresden.
And with the windows closed, they took us into-- towards the polish border.
We found out later it was the Polish border.
We arrived there in the middle of the night.
They stopped the train.
It was a-- it was a rainy, October night, and everybody had to get off the train.
And they said, keep on.
March on.
And all of a sudden, we found out that we were in no man's land.
The Germans had withdrawn.
And some young fellows in our group, they marched on to find out where we were.
And they came against-- across some Polish border patrols, and they didn't know what to do with us.
And we had to stay there until next morning.
And then finally, they got orders to let us in, and they took us to the nearest town.
And from there, we were put on trains to different parts in-- in Poland.
